tests.name: Writing — explain HTTP caching for non-technical readers

judge.model_response:
Imagine you are researching a topic in a massive, sprawling library. Every time you need a specific fact, you have to walk all the way to the back of the building, find the correct shelf, and carry a heavy book back to your desk. If you had to do this for every single sentence you read, you would spend much more time walking than actually studying.

Now, imagine that instead of walking back every time, you simply take a photocopy of the most important pages and keep them right on your study desk
judge.rationale: The response is exceptionally clear and tailored for a non-technical audience. It is technically accurate within the scope of a high-level explanation. It adheres to all constraints: the word count is approximately 210 words (well under the 250-word limit), it uses a highly effective library analogy, and it avoids technical jargon.

tests.raw_response:
Imagine you are researching a topic in a massive, sprawling library. Every time you need a specific fact, you have to walk all the way to the back of the building, find the correct shelf, and carry a heavy book back to your desk. If you had to do this for every single sentence you read, you would spend much more time walking than actually studying.

Now, imagine that instead of walking back every time, you simply take a photocopy of the most important pages and keep them right on your study desk. The next time you need that information, you just glance down. You didn't have to leave your seat, and you got your answer much faster.

This is exactly how website caching works. When you visit a website, your computer "photocopies" certain parts of the page—like the logo or the background images—and stores them on your device. The next time you visit that same site, your computer checks its own "desk" first. If it already has a recent copy, it displays the page instantly without having to request the data from the far-away website all over again. This makes your browsing experience much faster and smoother.

tests.raw_response:
Source C provides the most directly applicable evidence for a startup's decision because it evaluates the specific outcomes of a hybrid model compared to in-office and remote arrangements [C]. 

The source recommends a hybrid model—typically two to three days in the office—to capture the "measurable output advantages of remote work" while "preserving enough in-person interaction to sustain collaboration and culture" [C]. Highlighting a randomized controlled trial, Source C notes that a hybrid schedule (three days remote, two in office) resulted in productivity levels similar to remote work, but with significantly higher scores in collaboration quality, innovation contribution, and job satisfaction [C]. Additionally, the study found that voluntary attrition dropped 35 percent in the hybrid group [C]. To maximize benefits, the source suggests designating in-office days around collaborative tasks and ensuring team members' schedules overlap [C].

tests.raw_response:
The evidence regarding remote work's impact on productivity is multifaceted. Some research indicates a net gain for knowledge workers, noting increased call completion rates [A], higher hourly output per worker [A], and reduced turnover [A]. These gains are often attributed to fewer distractions and reduced commute fatigue [A].

Conversely, other data suggests remote work can fragment team networks and reduce exposure to novel information, potentially slowing innovation [B]. Challenges include diminished mentorship for junior employees [B], reduced employee belonging [B], and lower productivity in highly creative or junior roles lacking dedicated workspaces [A].

Hybrid models, specifically two to three days in-office, appear to balance these effects [C]. Research shows hybrid arrangements can maintain productivity levels similar to remote work while significantly improving collaboration quality, innovation, and job satisfaction [C]. Furthermore, effective hybrid productivity depends on task alignment—using remote settings for deep work and in-person time for brainstorming [C]—and ensuring team members overlap in the office [C].
